title: Continuous-variable ADAPT-VQE for bosonic lattice models
authors: Dimitrios Athanasakos, Gloria Tejedor-García, Jack Y. Araz, Mafalda Ramôa, Bharath Sambasivam, Sophia E. Economou, Felix Ringer
abstract: We present a continuous-variable adaptive variational quantum eigensolver (CV-ADAPT-VQE). As concrete examples, we consider the ground-state preparation for (i) the Bose-Hubbard model and (ii) the bosonic Kitaev chain, including its extension with an on-site Kerr interaction. The former conserves the total boson number, while the latter conserves global parity. We construct symmetry-preserving operator pools tailored to each case and show, using GPU-based classical simulations, that CV-ADAPT-VQE results in significantly shallower circuits compared to Hamiltonian-based VQE approaches. Our results point toward direct applications in quantum simulations of condensed-matter systems, quantum chemistry, and high-energy physics.
summary: The paper presents Continuous-variable ADAPT-VQE to find ground states for bosonic lattice models like the Bose-Hubbard and Kitaev chains. It demonstrates that this continuous-variable approach generates significantly shallower quantum circuits than standard VQE methods. By incorporating symmetry constraints and advanced optimization techniques, it offers a powerful tool for simulating complex condensed matter physics problems.
main_problem: The primary goal is to efficiently prepare the ground state of bosonic lattice models using quantum algorithms, specifically addressing circuit depth limitations.
main_result: The Continuous-variable ADAPT-VQE approach was shown to yield significantly shallower circuits compared to traditional Hamiltonian-based VQE methods for these systems.
method: The authors employ CV-ADAPT-VQE, an adaptive variational quantum eigensolver that constructs the ansatz by selecting symmetry-preserving bosonic operators from a tailored pool. This is contrasted with HVA-CV-VQE, which uses unitaries derived directly from the Hamiltonian terms.
model_or_system: The study focuses on bosonic lattice models, including the Bose-Hubbard (BH) model and the bosonic Kitaev chain (BKC), sometimes extended with an on-site Kerr interaction. These systems are modeled using continuous variables described by quadrature operators ($\hat{q}, \hat{p}$).
key_observables: Ground state energy expectation value ($E$), circuit depth, fidelity ($I$), and the total boson number $\langle \hat{N}_{tot} angle$.
important_parameters: System size ($N_S$), local Fock cutoff dimension ($D$), interaction strengths ($J, U, \Delta$), and chemical potential ($\mu$).
main_assumptions: Classical simulations require truncating the infinite Hilbert space to a finite dimension $D$. The ADAPT-VQE relies on constructing an ansatz from operators respecting known global symmetries.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate energy error ($\Delta E$) and infidelity ($I$) convergence versus gate count for BH and BKC models, comparing standard vs. tiled operator pools, and showing the impact of warm-up circuits and Hessian recycling on optimization steps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces CV-ADAPT-VQE for bosonic systems (BH/BKC), details the mathematical framework using continuous variables, compares its performance against HVA-CV-VQE, and discusses advanced optimizations like operator tiling and Hessian recycling to improve resource scaling.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical implementation challenges of simulating interacting quantum many-body systems on near-term quantum hardware by optimizing the required circuit depth using adaptive techniques tailored for bosonic Hamiltonians.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic illustration of the CV-ADAPT-VQE ground state preparation algorithm presented in this work. The state after n iterations is denoted by |ψ(n)⟩, which depends on the variational parameters of n gates, collectively denoted by ϑ (n). Depending on the lattice Hamiltonian, we choose a suitable operator pool that incorporates sym- metry constraints of the ground state.

FIG. 2: Expectation value of the total boson number across the lattice ⟨ˆNtot⟩for the ground state of the BKC model without (left) and with an additional on-site Kerr interaction (right). We show the result for different values of the model parameters ∆and µ for a lattice with NS = 3 sites, J = 1, U = −1 for the interaction term, periodic boundary conditions, and a local Hilbert space truncation of D = 6. The dashed golden line in the left panel indicates the stability condition, while the markers × and ◦denote the parameter choices (∆= 0.5, µ = −3.1) and (∆= 0.5, µ = −2.7), respectively, which are used for the variational ground state preparation presented in section V.

FIG. 3: Hamiltonian-based VQE ans¨atze for the Bose-Hubbard model (left) and the bosonic Kitaev chain (right) for NS = 3 lattice sites and periodic boundary conditions. For the bosonic Kitaev chain with an additional on-site Kerr interaction, another set of corresponding single-qumode Kerr gates is included previous to the rotation gates.

FIG. 4: The energy difference between the variational energy and the exact ground-state energy ∆E (solid lines, left axis), and the infidelity I (dashed lines, right axis), is shown as a function of the number of variational gates for the BH model. Panels (a) and (b) show the results for the attractive (ΛBH = +4.0) and repulsive (ΛBH = −4.0) BH models, respectively, for NS = 4 lattice sites and Ntot = 12 bosons with a local qumode cutoff of D = 13. As an example, in panel (a), we also plot the magnitude of the gradient associated with the gate selected by ADAPT |gr,init| (gray dotted line), i.e., the gate with the largest gradient at each iteration, using the scale of the left axis for...

FIG. 5: The energy difference between the variational energy and the exact ground-state energy (solid lines, left axis), together with the infidelity (dashed lines, right axis), for the HVA-CV-VQE (blue) and CV-ADAPT-VQE (red) algorithms as a function of the number of gates for the BKC model. The model parameter choices for the two rows are µ = −3.1 (top) and µ = −2.7 (bottom), which are in the unstable and stable regimes, respectively, see Fig. 2. In each row, the number of lattice sites NS increases from 3 to 5 (left to right). For all simulations, the local qumode cutoff is D = 6.
